summary: Do not ship: P-7 itself is resolved and the committed RED witness remains sound, but the class sweep still contains a no-op recovery test and unobserved post-park failures. Simpler alternative: none for P-7 beyond the implemented bounded await. Open question: none.

findings.title: T-B5④ discards the async grave operation without executing it
findings.body: `PassGuard::grave` is async, but `let _ = pass.grave(..)` drops its unpolled future. No blob-to-grave rename occurs, so after `drop(pass)` the next pass can find the original healthy blob and let the test pass even if `recover_graves` is broken. The intended fail-closed witness therefore proves nothing.
findings.recommendation: Await `pass.grave(&sha)`, assert success and the pre-recovery disk state (one grave and no canonical blob), discard the resulting `Graved`, then drop `pass` and run recovery.

findings.title: The intentionally parked put handles still swallow teardown failures
findings.body: The sweep exempts T-P4a and T-P4b-1/-2 because it claims no code executes after the park. Elsewhere the plan explicitly says dropping the sender releases `recv()` and lets the commit closure finish. Rename/fsync, guard-drop, or task failures can therefore occur during teardown while the retained put handle remains unawaited, producing a GREEN test despite a panic or error—the exact trap this sweep claims to eliminate.
findings.recommendation: Keep each put handle, perform all permanent-stall witness assertions, then explicitly drop the park sender and await the handle under a timeout, unwrapping both `JoinError` and the inner put result. Apply this to T-P4a and both T-P4b variants without changing production hooks.

next_steps: Revise only these test choreographies; keep the seven-hook production model unchanged.
Repeat the scoped plan review without re-running RED or GREEN tests.
